title: Nail Polish on Cotton
stainChemistry: Nail polish is polish film polymer + plasticizer + pigment + solvent (ethyl acetate, butyl acetate, or acetone). On contact with fabric the solvent evaporates fast and the polish film hardens into a tough, pigmented film mechanically anchored in the fiber texture. Water, soap, and oxidizers do nothing to polish film — it needs its own solvent class to re-soften. Cotton is cellulose — tolerates acetone, amyl acetate, and IPA at room temp. THE critical caveat: acetone DESTROYS acetate and triacetate fibers (acetate dissolves in acetone literally within seconds). Blend content must be confirmed before reaching for acetone — cotton/acetate blends exist on summer dresses and older linings.
whyThisWorks: Acetone or amyl acetate re-liquefies the polish film film, releasing the pigment so it can transfer onto a catch cloth. Non-acetone nail polish remover (ethyl acetate) is the slower, fiber-friendlier alternative — it works, just needs more passes. NSD emulsifies residual plasticizer. 3% H₂O₂ clears any residual dye shadow on whites. The rule: verify fiber (pure cotton vs cotton/acetate blend) BEFORE acetone.
defaultAssumption: Pure cotton is a safe bet for acetone; assume pure cotton unless the label says otherwise. Cotton blends (especially with acetate or triacetate) narrow the solvent choice to non-acetone remover. Heat-set polish (already hot-washed or dried) trends toward escalation.

spottingProtocol.technique: Label read, catch cloth under stain
spottingProtocol.equipment: Care label, clean white cotton towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Read the care label. 100% cotton: acetone is safe. Cotton/acetate or cotton/triacetate blends: acetone dissolves the acetate fiber — use non-acetone (ethyl acetate) nail polish remover instead. Unknown blend: default to non-acetone. Then place a folded clean white towel UNDER the stain — the dissolved polish transfers downward into the catch cloth, not sideways.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull blade, spoon edge, or credit card
spottingProtocol.instruction: If the polish has dried into a raised film, scrape the brittle top layer off with a dull blade. Don't rub — rubbing grinds the polymer into the weave. Lift and flake. This reduces the mass that the solvent has to dissolve.

spottingProtocol.technique: Solvent on white cloth, tamp from back
spottingProtocol.equipment: Acetone (pure cotton only) OR amyl acetate OR non-acetone polish remover on a clean white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply the solvent to a CLEAN white cloth — never to the fabric directly. Tamp the stain from the BACK of the fabric, over the catch cloth. The polymer re-softens and transfers onto the cloths. Flip the pressing cloth to a clean section frequently. Expect 3–5 passes — deeper polish layers surface as each pass lifts the outer layer.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush. NSD emulsifies the residual plasticizer the solvent freed. Rinse cool from the back.
For residual pigment shadow on whites: 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop. Oxidizes the dye color. Dwell 3–5 min, blot. One oxidizer at a time. Skip on dark or dyed cotton.
Rinse thoroughly. Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer — dryer heat sets any residual polish permanently.

steps: 1. Verify fiber. Pure cotton = acetone safe. Cotton/acetate blend = acetone DESTROYS; use amyl acetate or non-acetone remover.
2. Place catch cloth UNDER the stain.
4. Acetone (pure cotton) OR amyl acetate (universal) OR non-acetone remover on a white cloth, tamp from the back. 3–5 passes.
5. NSD to emulsify residual plasticizer. Cool rinse.
6. 3% H₂O₂ on residual dye shadow (whites only). 3–5 min.
7. Hot wash per care label. Inspect before dryer.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: ACETONE DESTROYS ACETATE AND TRIACETATE — verify fiber before use
Always tamp with a catch cloth under + pressing cloth on top — prevents sideways spread
One oxidizer at a time; skip peroxide on dark/dyed cotton
Ventilate — acetone and amyl acetate are flammable

homeSolutions.instruction: This stain has ONE rule that matters above all others: if the fabric contains acetate or triacetate (common in summer dresses, linings, and some blends), acetone-based nail polish remover will DISSOLVE the fiber literally within seconds. Pull the garment inside out and read the care label. 100% cotton: acetone remover is fine. If it says acetate/triacetate or blend: use non-acetone remover only. Unknown: use non-acetone to be safe.

homeSolutions.agent: Place a White Towel UNDER the Stain
homeSolutions.instruction: Fold a clean white towel and put it UNDER the stained fabric. When the polish dissolves in the next step, it needs somewhere to go — the catch cloth pulls it out. Skip this and the polish smears sideways into clean fabric.
If the polish has dried into a hard film, use the edge of a credit card or a dull butter knife to lift the top layer. Lift, don't rub. You're reducing how much the remover has to dissolve.

homeSolutions.agent: Nail Polish Remover on a White Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab nail polish remover onto a clean white cloth — not onto the fabric directly. Press the cloth onto the stain from the BACK of the fabric, over the catch cloth. Flip the pressing cloth to a clean spot every 10 seconds. The polish transfers out as the pigment dissolves. Expect 3–5 rounds. Ventilate the room — open a window, no flames, no smoking.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Acetone-based remover on ACETATE (common in summer dresses and linings) dissolves the fabric. Read the care label first. Non-acetone remover is the safe default.

homeSolutions.instruction: Work a drop of Dawn dish soap into the spot with a soft toothbrush. Dawn lifts the plasticizer residue the solvent left behind. Rinse cool.

homeSolutions.agent: Hydrogen Peroxide 3% for Faint Color (Whites Only)
homeSolutions.instruction: For a residual pink or red shadow on white cotton: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide, let it bubble 3–5 min, blot. Skip on dark or dyed cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia — toxic fumes.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. If you still see polish, repeat the remover + Dawn round first. Dryer heat locks in any residual polymer film.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Acetate Damage or Heat-Set
homeSolutions.instruction: If acetone was accidentally used on an acetate-blend fabric, the fiber damage is not recoverable at home. Take to a dry cleaner immediately. If nail polish has been hot-dried onto cotton or two home rounds haven't cleared it, same answer. Tell them: "Nail polish on [cotton / cotton blend / acetate], age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with [acetone / non-acetone remover] + Dawn. [Fiber intact / fiber damage suspected]."

steps: 1. READ THE LABEL. 100% cotton: acetone OK. Any acetate blend: non-acetone remover only.
2. Place catch cloth UNDER the stain.
3. Scrape dried polish gently with a credit card edge.
4. Nail polish remover on a clean white cloth, tamp from the BACK. Flip cloth frequently. 3–5 passes. Ventilate.
5. Dawn + soft toothbrush for residual plasticizer.
6. 3% hydrogen peroxide on residual pigment shadow (whites only).
7. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
8. Escalate immediately if acetone hit acetate, or if heat-set.
products: Non-acetone nail polish remover (ethyl acetate — the safe default)
Acetone-based nail polish remover (100% cotton only)
Clean white cloths for catch + pressing
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop immediately if acetone was applied to an acetate-blend fabric (fiber damage is done; take to a pro). Stop if two home rounds haven't cleared the polish, or if the cotton is dark dyed and peroxide isn't safe.
warnings: ACETONE DESTROYS ACETATE FIBER — read the label before you reach for remover
Solvent on cloth, NOT on fabric — tamp, never pour
Inspect before the dryer — heat sets residual polymer

neverDo: Never use acetone on acetate / triacetate fiber — destroys the fiber
Never pour solvent onto fabric — tamp with cloth, catch cloth underneath
Never skip the catch cloth — sideways pigment spread
Never use near open flame — acetone is flammable
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia
fiberSensitivities: Pure cotton: tolerates acetone, amyl acetate, ethyl acetate at room temp
Dyed cotton: test acetone on inside seam — some dyes lift
Heat-durable fiber — hot wash is the finisher

materialWarnings: Verify the fiber label BEFORE reaching for acetone
Catch cloth UNDER + tamp on TOP, always

escalation.when: Acetone was applied to an acetate-blend fabric (fiber damage), polish has been hot-dried into cotton, two home rounds haven't cleared it, or dark dyed cotton where peroxide isn't safe.
escalation.whatToTell: "Nail polish on [100% cotton / cotton-acetate blend / unsure], age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with [acetone-based / non-acetone] remover + Dawn. [Fiber intact / fiber damage suspected / hot-washed]."

contextQuestions: What does the care label say — 100% cotton or blended with acetate?
How old is the stain — fresh, overnight, older?
Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The #1 home failure on this stain is acetone on an acetate blend — irreversible fiber damage within seconds. Label-read first is the non-negotiable rule. Non-acetone remover is the safe default when in doubt.
